coaching.title: Today's Run
coaching.message: Easy 45–50 minutes in Z2 (130–140 bpm), no intervals, no hills. You're in taper week 20, and your recovery score is 46—moderate but not alarming. Your resting HR is stable at 63, which is good. Thursday is your traditional recovery day, and with 27 miles already logged this week, you need to preserve legs for tomorrow's work and Saturday's long run. Keep it conversational, flat terrain if possible. This run serves one purpose: maintain aerobic conditioning without accumulating fatigue.

coaching.title: Fuel Right
coaching.message: You slept 5.7 hours last night—that's well below your 30-day average of 4.67h, which actually means you got a genuinely decent night. Don't squander it. Eat a full breakfast with 60–80g carbs and 20g protein within an hour of waking. During your easy run, skip fuel if it's under 60 minutes. Post-run, consume 30–40g carbs and 15g protein within 30 minutes to initiate glycogen resynthesis. Given the taper phase, your total daily intake should trend toward maintenance (not elevated), but nutrient timing matters more than ever—you're sharpening, not building.

coaching.title: Recovery
coaching.message: Your 5.7-hour sleep was solid; aim for 7–8 hours tonight. The Julian Alps 50K demands sustained neuromuscular function over 7–9 hours of variable terrain—sleep is where adaptation happens. Prioritize darkness, no screens after 21:00, and a cool bedroom (65–68°F). Your recovery score of 46 suggests mild fatigue accumulation; use today's easy run and tomorrow's lower volume to let your CNS reset. Elevation in alpine terrain magnifies the cost of poor sleep, so sleep is a non-negotiable training tool, not a luxury.

coaching.title: Mental Edge
coaching.message: You're 99 days out—deep in the taper, where momentum feels like it's slipping but sharpness is actually crystallizing. This is when doubt creeps in. Reframe today: easy volume is intentional precision, not coasting. Review your recent 9-miler at 141 bpm (hard effort) and your 3-miler at 118 bpm (recovery)—your ranges are dialed. The Julian Alps will punish sloppy pacing and poor fueling; trust that your 20 weeks of work is locked in. Use these final nine weeks to refine strategy (aid station timing, descent technique, gear) rather than chase fitness gains that won't materialize.
